Rotator cuff tears
Asheesh Bedi 1,2 , Julie Bishop 3 , Jay Keener 4 , Drew A. Lansdown 5 , Ofer Levy 6 , Peter MacDonald 7 , Nicola Maffulli
Joo Han Oh 9 , Vani J. Sabesan 10 , Joaquin Sanchez-Sotelo 11 , Riley J. Williams III 12  & Brian T. Feeley
Abstract
Rotator cuff tears are the most common upper extremity condition seen
by primary care and orthopaedic surgeons, with a spectrum ranging
from tendinopathy to full-thickness tears with arthritic change. Some
tears are traumatic, but most rotator cuff problems are degenerative.
Not all tears are symptomatic and not all progress, and many patients
in whom tears become more extensive do not experience symptom
worsening. Hence, a standard algorithm for managing patients is
challenging. The pathophysiology of rotator cuff tears is complex
and encompasses an interplay between the tendon, bone and muscle.
Rotator cuff tears begin as degenerative changes within the tendon,
with matrix disorganization and inflammatory changes. Subsequently,
tears progress to partial-thickness and then full-thickness tears. Muscle
quality, as evidenced by the overall size of the muscle and intramuscular
fatty infiltration, also influences symptoms, tear progression and the
outcomes of surgery. Treatment depends primarily on symptoms, with
non-operative management sufficient for most patients with rotator
cuff problems. Modern arthroscopic repair techniques have improved
recovery, but outcomes are still limited by a lack of understanding of
how to improve tendon to bone healing in many patients.

Epidemiology
Prevalence
Patients can develop shoulder pain at any point along the spectrum
of rotator cuff tears. Most patients with a rotator cuff tear do not have
symptoms of pain or weakness and do not seek medical care. Hence,
the exact prevalence of rotator cuff disease is unknown. Although the
incidence of symptomatic rotator cuff disease is probably steady, rota-
tor cuff repair surgery is more common than 20 years ago, probably
due to the increased use of arthroscopic techniques and transition
to outpatient surgery. One study showed an annual increase in rota-
tor cuff surgery of 1.6% per year in the USA between 2007 and 2016
(165 repairs per 100,000 person-years 10 ). In the absence of pain, indi-
viduals with rotator cuff tears have been shown to maintain nearly nor-
mal shoulder function 11 . Population screening studies using shoulder
ultrasonography have better defined the prevalence of rotator cuff
tear 12 . A cross-sectional study involving 1,000 women in the UK demon-
strated that 22.2% had a full-thickness rotator cuff tear. Advancing age
and dominant shoulder involvement were positively correlated with
the presence of a tear. Similarly, two different observational studies
in Asian populations showed a prevalence of full-thickness cuff tears
of 20.7% (1,366 individuals) and 22.1% (664 individuals), with higher
prevalence in individuals ≥57 years of age than in younger individuals
and in men than in women 13 , 14 .
Risk factors
Age .  The overwhelming clinical evidence suggests that the primary
risk factor for the development of rotator cuff degeneration is age 4 , 5 , 15 , 16 .
Multiple clinical studies have shown that patients with a painful rotator
cuff tear often have an asymptomatic degenerative tear in the opposite
shoulder 5 , 11 , 17 , 18 . One study performed bilateral shoulder ultrasonog-
raphy on patients presenting with unilateral shoulder pain, and dem-
onstrated a strong association between cuff disease and ageing 19 . The
presence of a full-thickness tear in the painful shoulder was associated
with a 21% risk of a partial-thickness tear and 36% risk of a full-thickness
tear in the contralateral shoulder. The average age of those with an
intact cuff, a unilateral cuff tear and bilateral partial-thickness or
full-thickness cuff tears demonstrated a nearly perfect 10-year distri-
bution (48.7, 58.7 and 67.8 years of age, respectively) 17 . Patients with a
full-thickness cuff tear on the symptomatic side had a 50% chance of
having a concomitant cuff tear on the asymptomatic side at ≥66 years
of age. One study involving pooled data from 30 separate studies and
>6,000 shoulders demonstrated a strong correlation between the pres-
ence of a tear and increasing age stratified by decade, with a prevalence
of 9.7% in those <20 years of age compared with a prevalence of 62% in
those >80 years of age 20 .
Genetics .  Genetics may play a role in the development of rotator cuff
disease (Box  2 ). One study demonstrated a familial association of
rotator cuff disease, noting that siblings were twice as likely as spouses
to develop a full-thickness cuff tear, regardless of symptoms, and
more than four times as likely to develop a painful full-thickness tear
compared with the general population 21 , 22 . Additionally, progression
of tear size at 5-year follow-up was greater in siblings than controls 23 .
Studies have shown an association between variants in genes related to
cellular apoptosis on chromosome 6 and chromosome 17 and rotator
cuff disease 24 . Indeed, genome-wide association studies have shown
variants in multiple candidate genes using large population-based
genetic databases 25 , 26 . RNA sequencing of the UK Biobank showed an
increased expression of  STK24  and a decreased expression of  SAT1
Introduction
The rotator cuff is a group of muscles and tendons that maintains the
shoulder in place and provides dynamic motion and stability to
the joint. It is composed of four separate muscles, namely the supraspi-
natus, infraspinatus, subscapularis and teres minor. The tendons from
these four muscles coalesce at their attachments on the humeral head
(Fig.  1 ). The glenohumeral joint has the greatest range of motion of
any joint in the human body, and inherently relies upon the rotator
cuff to provide dynamic stabilization as well as function to position
the arm and hand.
Rotator cuff tears are the most common upper extremity con-
dition seen by primary care physicians, physiatrists and orthopae-
dic surgeons 1 . Rotator cuff injuries present as a spectrum of disease
ranging from tendinopathy and partial-thickness tears, extending to
full-thickness tears with progressive cartilage degeneration (cuff tear
arthropathy) 2  (Box  1  and Fig.  2 ). Symptoms of rotator cuff injuries are
broad and can range from minimal symptoms including difficulty
sleeping and pain during overhead reaching to loss of active and passive
motion with progressive shoulder girdle weakness and dysfunction.
Although some tears are traumatic, most rotator cuff problems
are degenerative in nature due, in part, to age-related changes to the
­tendon structure. The leading hypothesis is that tears progress from
tendinopathic changes in a watershed area that lacks blood supply
in the supraspinatus to partial-thickness tears and eventually full-
thickness tears 3 , which are accompanied by progressive muscle and
tendon degeneration. Confounding the natural history is that not all
tears progress and/or worsen, and in many patients who present with a
progressive increase in the tear size, symptoms do not worsen, making
a standard algorithm for treatment challenging 4 , 5 .
The pathophysiology of rotator cuff tears is complex, as it involves
the interplay between the tendon, bone and muscle. A key to under -
standing rotator cuff pathology and tearing is delineating the role of
tendon and muscle degeneration. Increasing evidence suggests that
the mTOR pathway might have an important role in the regulation of
tendon degeneration over time 6 . Muscle quality, as evidenced by the
overall size of the muscle, as well as intramuscular fat deposition (fatty
infiltration) also influences shoulder strength, tear progression and
the outcome of surgery 7 . Studies also highlight the role of fibroadipo-
progenitor cells (FAPs) (muscle-resident multipotent mesenchymal
stem cells (MSCs)) as a central mediator of muscle degeneration 8 , 9 .
The management of rotator cuff tears is generally based on
symptoms. Indeed, most patients with rotator cuff tears diagnosed
on advanced imaging (for example, MRI or ultrasonography) do not
require surgery. Observation, physical therapy and corticosteroid
injections, as well as surgical repair and reconstruction of the rotator
cuff, all play a role in patient recovery, which depends on many patient-
specific factors such as age, activity level and hand dominance, and
intrinsic factors of the rotator cuff.
Of note, the terminology of rotator cuff disease in the literature
varies: a rotator cuff problem is described as a tendinopathy, a tear,
an injury or rotator cuff disease. Thus, the nomenclature leads to
­confusion for the patient and the provider.
In this Primer, we present the current knowledge of the basic and
clinical science of rotator cuff disease, highlighting the clinical pres-
entation, natural history, risk factors and current treatment options.
Furthermore, we provide an evidence-based treatment algorithm and
consolidate the unmet needs in the field as well as outline the barriers
necessary to overcome the modest improvements in outcomes of
surgery over the past 20 years.

Comorbidities .  Several disease conditions have been postulated to
have a role in the development and progression of rotator cuff disease,
and subsequently, may influence healing rates following surgical repair.
These conditions include type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidaemia, sex
hormone deficiency, obesity, smoking, hypertension, gout, connective
tissue diseases and depression 23 , 29 – 33 . Diabetes mellitus promotes the
development of tendinopathy in several areas, including the rotator
cuff and Achilles tendon, perhaps owing to altered neural, vascular and
immune responses to tissue remodelling 3 . Samples of torn rotator cuff
tendons from patients with diabetes mellitus show higher expression
of matrix metalloproteinases, such as MMP2, and higher expression of
inflammatory proteins (IL-6) than samples from individuals without
diabetes 34 . Studies have demonstrated that diabetes mellitus is a definite
risk factor for impaired healing after rotator cuff surgery, and those
patients with a lower HbA 1c  (<7%) in the postoperative period have
lower retear rates than patients with higher HbA 1c  (>7%), suggesting
an important role of diabetes mellitus in controlling tendon to bone
healing 35 , 36 . Evidence also shows that there is an association between
oestrogen deficiency in women and testosterone deficiency in men and
outcomes following surgery for a rotator cuff tear 37 . Similarly, in an insur-
ance database study, women with oestrogen deficiency were 48% more
likely and men with testosterone deficiency were 89% more likely than
age-matched controls to undergo rotator cuff repair surgery 38 . The exact
mechanism of hormone deficiencies and their contribution to rotator
cuff tears, at the cellular level, have not been elucidated at this point.
Physical activity and occupation .  The role of physical activity in
the development of rotator cuff disease is debated. Studies in rats
have shown that excessive physical activity promotes supraspinatus
tendinopathy. The changes induced in the tendon include increased
tendon hypertrophy and decreased elasticity and were associated
with a combination of intrinsic and extrinsic factors (compression
from the coracoacromial arch) 39 , 40 . By contrast, one study failed to
show an association between the level of overhead physical activity or
occupational demands with tear progression in a cohort of patients with

of a symptomatic tear in the dominant shoulder than the non-dominant
shoulder 13 , 41 – 43 . Occupational demands might play a part in rotator cuff
tear development; for example, studies have identified several occu-
pational exposure risk factors, such as repetitive arm elevation, heavy
lifting and use of vibrating tools, that are associated with rotator cuff
tears 44 – 48 . However, the exact duration and type of exposure that con-
stitute a risk is unknown. A study in a population of >250,000 individu-
als (UK Biobank) showed a correlation between self-reported manual
labour and the need for rotator cuff surgery compared with ­controls,
particularly when the occupational exposure lasted >10 years 49 .
Natural history
Although, the greatest risk factor for rotator cuff tear development is
age, time is the greatest risk factor for tear progression. The risk of tear
enlargement is greater in full-thickness tears than partial-thickness
tears 5 , 43 , 50 . Although smoking, obesity, diabetes and hyperlipidaemia
have been linked to rotator cuff disease, no studies have examined
the effects of these conditions on the natural history or progression
of rotator cuff disease. The role of scapular morphology, specifically
the acromion, in the development of and the progression of degenera-
tive rotator cuff tears is debated. For example, in one study, the critical
shoulder angle of patients with an asymptomatic rotator cuff tear was
larger than in control patients with normal rotator cuffs but was not
associated with tear size or the risk of tear enlargement (median time
to enlargement of 3.3 years) 51 . Other studies, including a meta-analysis,
demonstrated considerable variability in the relationship between criti-
cal shoulder angle and the presence of rotator cuff tears, suggesting
that it may only be a factor in some patients 52 .
The typical site of initiation of a rotator cuff tear is between
13 mm and 17 mm posterior to the biceps tendon within the rotator
crescent 16 , 53 . The rotator crescent is a relatively hypovascular area of
the posterior cuff that is protected to some degree biomechanically
by the thick rotator cable, which spans from the anterior edge of the
supraspinatus to the inferior infraspinatus 54 – 56 . Approximately 30% of
full-thickness tears, regardless of tear size, involve the anterior cable
tissue adjacent to the biceps tendon, and only 3% of small full-thickness
tears (≤10 mm) involve this area 53 , 57 . Longitudinal studies have shown
that tear enlargement within the rotator crescent is common in the
anterior and posterior directions 4 . Rotator cuff tear location mapping
has shown that anterior cable disruption is strongly associated with
supraspinatus muscle degeneration 16 , 58 .
Pain is an important clinical indicator of tear size progression
over time with the tear width and amount of retraction from the bone
increasing. Much of the research examining tear progression has been
performed in asymptomatic shoulders by identifying painless tears in
patients presenting with a painful cuff tear in the opposite shoulder.
Clinically, most patients in whom the tear progressed had an increased
risk of developing pain 59 . In a large study of 224 shoulders in patients
with asymptomatic tears in one shoulder and a contralateral painful
rotator cuff tear, tear progression was found in 49% of asymptomatic
shoulders by ultrasonography, with a median time to enlargement of
2.8 years 5 . In this study, the risk of tear enlargement for full-thickness
tears was significantly greater than for partial-thickness tears and
control shoulders. Overall, 46% of originally asymptomatic shoulders
developed pain at a median of 2.6 years. A subsequent study involv-
ing 227 shoulders in patients aged 65 years or younger demonstrated
continued tear progression over an average follow up of 7.1 years 43 .
Overall, 60% of tears enlarged during the 7-year study period. The risks
of enlargement of a full-thickness tear at 2 years, 5 years and 8 years
were 26%, 58% and 80%, respectively. Approximately 57% of the cohort
developed pain, which was strongly linked to tear enlargement.
The presence and progression of degeneration of the supraspinatus
and infraspinatus muscles were linked to absolute tear size and tear
­enlargement during follow-up 43 .
Clinical evidence strongly suggests that symptomatic degenera-
tive full-thickness rotator cuff tears do not heal, and most tears are at
risk of slow progression over time. Tear progression can be defined
as tear enlargement or progression of muscle degeneration — both of
which have implications on healing potential following surgical repair.
Multiple studies have demonstrated that painful tears will enlarge in
size, especially over a period of 18 months or more, but the amount
of tear enlargement is relatively low 50 , 60 . For example, in 49 patients
with a symptomatic full-thickness rotator cuff tear, the average tear
size increase was <1 cm, and progression of muscle degeneration was
seen in <50% of patients 59 .
Box 1
Classification of rotator cuff
tears
Tears are classified based on their size (partial or complete), and
several classification systems are used. Most commonly, partial
tears are classified based on the amount of tearing off the footprint
(Ellman classification) as seen on MRI. Full thickness tears are
usually classified with the Codman classification based on tear size
in the anterior–posterior dimension. Although these classifications
lack high reproducibility, the Codman classification is predictive
of outcome after rotator cuff repair, and helps guide treatment
decisions. The Goutallier classification is utilized to determine the
degree of fatty infiltration within the rotator cuff, and is assessed
on sagittal views on MRI.
Partial-thickness tears — Ellman classification 301
•  Grade 1: <3 mm (<25% thickness)
•  Grade 2: 3–6 mm (25–50% thickness)
•  Grade 3: >6 mm (>50% thickness)
•  Location: A, articular sided; B, bursal sided; C, intratendinous
Full-thickness tears — Codman classification (tear size
in anterior to posterior direction) 302
•  Small: 0–1 cm
•  Medium: 1–3 cm
•  Large: 3–5 cm
•  Massive: >5 cm
Fatty infiltration — Goutallier classification 160
•  Normal: score 0
•  Fatty streaks: score 1
•  More muscle than fat: score 2
•  Equal fat and muscle: score 3
•  More fat than muscle: score 4

Rotator cuff muscle degeneration refers to the development
of muscle atrophy and fatty cell infiltration into the muscle. Rotator
cuff muscle degeneration is almost exclusive to full-thickness rotator cuff
tears 16 , 57 . In one study, tears that enlarged had more than twice the risk
of progression of muscle degeneration than stable tears 61 . Additionally,
when tear progression led to disruption of the anterior cable or when
the extent of tear enlargement was big, the risk of progression of muscle
degeneration increased over time after adjusting for covariates 16 , 61 .
Mechanisms/pathophysiology
Rotator cuff tears represent a spectrum of disease, from impingement,
tendinopathy, to partial-thickness tears and to full-thickness tears of
varying sizes (Fig.  2 ). Impingement (originally named after the idea
that the acromion impinges on the rotator cuff 62 ) occurs when the
bursa above the rotator cuff is inflamed as a consequence of altered
kinematics of the shoulder girdle. When the larger muscles (deltoid)
over-compensate due to rotator cuff dysfunction, the rotator cuff
cannot function as an effective force couple, resulting in the humeral
head elevating rather than depressing during shoulder motion 63 , 64 .
This altered movement pattern increases inflammation within the
rotator cuff bursa above the tendon, leading to pain with motion and
overhead activity, and pain during sleep. Although imaging findings
are normal in patients with mild impingement, increased fluid signal
on ultrasonography or MRI above the rotator cuff is a key indication of
active impingement. Studies have suggested that the bursa is a source
of active healing of the cuff, by stimulating inflammatory cells and fac-
tors that promote healing 65 – 67 . The bursal cells display mesenchymal
markers of stemness, including CD73 and CD90, and, in experimental
models of bursa–tendon crosstalk, the bursa has upregulated markers
of extracellular matrix production (TGFβ), immune cell signalling and
vascular signalling including PDGFRβ 68 .
The exact mechanisms of how rotator cuff injury progresses from
tendinopathy to partial-thickness tears and to full-thickness tears is
yet to be fully understood. In addition, the inconsistent terminology
commonly used even among orthopaedic surgeons can be confus-
ing and misleading to patients and providers alike 69 , 70 . Patients who
present with new-onset pain, from a purely biological viewpoint, might
have well-established histopathological changes. Why a biologically
long-standing condition suddenly manifests itself in an acute fashion
is still unclear.
The present scientific evidence suggests that the initial lesion
of tendinopathy is a failed healing response to microtrauma, and the
role of inflammation in these conditions has been re-evaluated and
is thought to be an important contributor 71 , 72  (Fig.  3 ). In the devel -
opment of tendinopathy of the rotator cuff, an increase in inflam-
matory cells and production of pro-inflammatory cytokines from
tenocytes are thought to have a role 3 , 73 . Animal models of tendino­
pathy show increased numbers of macrophages and mast cells within
the injured tendon 74 . In response to altered mechanical load or
­tissue injury, tenocytes and immune cells within the tendon release
pro-inflammatory cytokines, including TNF, IL-6 and IL-1β. IL-17A has
been found to be upregulated in patients with a rotator cuff tear 75 .
IL-17A was expressed in various inflammatory cells within rotator cuff
tendon, and tenocytes that were treated with IL-17A had increased
production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and increased expres-
sion of apoptosis-related factors 75 . The role of IL-17A is notable given
the availability of anti-IL-17A monoclonal antibodies (secukinumab
and ixekizumab) that have been evaluated for their role in treating
tendinopathy 3 .
In most patients, partial-thickness tears and full-thickness tears
are hypothesized to be the result of the tendinopathy process over
a period of many years. Histologically, tendinopathy demonstrates
altered collagen organization with increased inflammatory tissue with
decreased vascularity, which, for as-yet unidentified reasons, may not
necessarily cause symptoms even as tendinopathy progresses into
partial-thickness and full-thickness tears. The severity of tear as well
as the extent of tendinopathy do not correlate with patient symptoms,
which highlights the complex relationship between degeneration,
pain development and pain perception at the central nervous system
level. An upregulation of the glutamatergic system has been observed
in patients with rotator cuff tendinopathy, with localization of glu-
tamate to tenocytes with glutamate receptors on macrophages 76 .

Further, other nociceptive mediators such as mGLuR2 and GluK1
are also upregulated in patients with tendinopathy 76 . Interestingly,
tenocytes exposed to glutamate in vitro show decreased cell viability
and decreased collagen gene expression 77 , providing a possible link
between pain and tendon degeneration.
Tendon degeneration
The cellular and molecular pathophysiology of degenerative tendino­
pathy is highly complex and is only partially understood. A myriad
of predisposing factors, including patient-specific factors such as
metabolic disorders (that is, hypercholesterolaemia, hyperglycae-
mia), mechanical overuse and repetitive microtrauma, drug exposure
and ischaemia contribute to profound structural changes within the
tendon 3 . Both animal-based and clinical studies have demonstrated that
activation of secondary inflammatory pathways and oxidative stress in
tendon tissue promote tenocyte apoptosis, fibrosis and disorganization
of the extracellular matrix and, in some cases, heterotopic ossification
(a condition in which bone tissue develops in soft tissue) 28 , 78 , 79 . Impor-
tantly, chronic tears are the result of consistent tendon abnormalities in
the tendon tissue that has been degenerated over a long time. Studies
have demonstrated tissue metaplasia, tenocyte apoptosis and reduced
regenerative properties of tendon-derived stem cells in tendinopathic
tendons, possibly secondary to inflammation and abnormal oxidative
stresses 80 . Anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory therapies that mitigate
or suppress these pathways might have therapeutic benefit by altering
the natural history of tendinopathy in these settings.
Oxidative stress .  Oxidative stress activates signalling pathways that
generate reactive oxygen species, which induce inflammation and in
excessive quantities, damage cells, lipids, proteins and even DNA 81 , 82 .
Clinical studies have shown increased levels of serum hydroperox-
ides in tendinopathic tendon tissue and increased concentrations
of inflammatory proteins in the synovium and tendon in rotator cuff
tears 28 . In animal models, increased mechanical loading and tendon
overuse increased the plasma total oxidant status and circulating
pro-inflammatory cytokines 79 . Multiple pathways are likely to be
involved, and activation of the mTOR pathway 6 , resulting in tissue
metaplasia and impaired tenogenesis of tendon-derived stem cells 83 ,
may have a major role in tendon degeneration and impaired tendon
healing. Interestingly, mTOR is also a central regulator of muscle atro-
phy; hence, targeting this pathway may serve a dual role in future
treatment strategies 84 .
Obesity, diabetes mellitus or hypercholesterolaemia further pre-
dispose to a pro-inflammatory environment and increase the risk for
degenerative tendinopathy. In individuals with comorbidities, reactive
oxidative species are increased in these settings, triggering tenocyte
apoptosis, fibrosis and tissue metaplasia via mTOR, nicotinamide dinu-
cleotide phosphate oxidase (NOX) and AKT/FOXO1 pathways 82 , 85 . Drugs
such as fluoroquinolones or local anaesthetics such as bupivacaine also
predispose to tendinopathy, probably by altering mitochondrial mem-
brane permeability and/or generating reactive oxygen species in cells,
thereby inducing oxidative stress and a catabolic, pro-inflammatory
response 86 , 87 .
Accordingly, anti-oxidants that reduce the production of reactive
oxidative species or drugs such as rapamycin, which inhibit down-
stream pathways (for example, mTOR), are being actively investi-
gated for managing symptomatic tendinopathy 6 . Studies examining
natural anti-oxidants such as curcumin, eugenol and flavonoids, are
ongoing 80 , 88 . Well-designed clinical studies assessing the efficacy of
these therapies in symptomatic patients are warranted.
Muscle quality
Muscle quality, defined by the development of muscle atrophy and
fatty infiltration, has been implicated as a key factor affecting the
outcomes of rotator cuff injury and repair (Fig.  3 ). Muscle atrophy,
a likely consequence of mechanical unloading of the tendon and fatty
infiltration, which seems to be chronic resulting from alterations in
innervation, affects rotator cuff treatment outcomes. Patients with
greater muscle atrophy and increased fatty infiltration are at higher
risk of retear after surgery, and worse clinical outcomes than those
with less muscle atrophy, even with a technically successful repair 7 , 89 .
Muscle atrophy .  Atrophy is defined as the loss of muscle mass, with
a physiological shift from protein synthesis to protein degradation 90 .
Atrophy is an independent factor that predicts outcomes after rotator
cuff repair. The degree of atrophy is used in combination with fatty
infiltration and the amount of tendon retraction to gauge the ‘repair-
ability’ of a rotator cuff tear. Full-thickness rotator cuff tears result in
alterations in mechanical load to the muscle–tendon unit, which nega-
tively affect key protein activation, including AKT and mTOR, and alter
the expression of atrophy-related genes (atrogenes), such as  MuRF1

Similarly, in human studies, satellite cell numbers are not diminished,
but their ability to contribute to muscle size and regeneration may be
limited in the setting of aged rotator cuff tissue 97 – 101 .
Fatty infiltration .  Fatty infiltration (that is, infiltration of fat from the
surrounding tissue 102 ) has emerged as an important biological concept
involved in muscle degeneration across many conditions, including
ageing, low back pain and muscular dystrophy. The presence of fatty
infiltration worsens the quality of muscle, as the amount of tissue
that can develop force decreases with a concomitant alteration in
the biomechanical properties of the tissue 103 . Several animal studies
and human studies have demonstrated the importance of FAPs in
the development of fatty infiltration 9 . These cells have a central role
in the regulation of muscle degeneration; for example, in muscular
dystrophy, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and ageing 104 – 111 . In animal
models, FAPs have been shown to proliferate at the time of rotator
cuff injury and directly contribute to the development of fatty infil-
tration through expression of white fat-associated genes including
PPARG ,  CREBP  and other fat-related genes 84 , 96 , 112 . Fatty infiltration can
be decreased through inhibition signalling molecules, such as TGFβ,
retinoic acid and PPARγ, which probably work through modulating
the response of FAPs to injury 113 – 116 . In human studies, FAPs prolifer-
ate at the time of rotator cuff injury, and increase in number as tear
size progresses 8  and express several fat-related and fibrosis-related
genes 117 – 119 . Non-biased transcriptomic studies have shown that FAPs
isolated from partial-thickness tears are less likely to express mark-
ers of adipogenesis and fibrosis than FAPs from full-thickness tears.
Adipogenic commitment is driven by downregulation of DLK1, which
is seen in FAPs from patients with full-thickness tears 120 . Importantly,
FAPs also seem to have pro-regenerative capabilities in vitro when
stimulated with pharmacological agents, which may be utilized to
improve muscle quality at the time of and after rotator cuff repair 121 – 124 .
Tendon healing after repair
Tendon–bone healing, a highly complex process, is crucial to the suc-
cess of rotator cuff repair; yet, the mechanism underlying this process
remains a critical unmet need in this field. The native enthesis is a highly
organized structure with remarkable architecture designed to transmit
loads from muscle through unmineralized and mineralized fibrocarti-
lage to bone 125 , 126 . Re-establishment of the rotator cuff tendon to bone
interface does not recapitulate the organized anatomy or structure of
the native enthesis. Lineage tracing studies suggest that even in acute
reattachment, the native enthesis is replaced with scar-forming cells
leading rather to a mechanically inferior attachment 127 .
Healing at the enthesis follows the typical reparative cascade of
inflammation, proliferation and remodelling 128 . Pro-inflammatory
cytokines released by platelets from the clot recruit macrophages,
neutrophils and tendon-derived cells to initiate the repair response.
The proliferative phase of collagen and extracellular matrix deposition
by recruited fibroblasts results in an increase in cellularity and collagen
deposition. During this time, there is also an increase in vascularity. This
is followed by remodelling, in which there is a subsequent reduction of
cellularity and vascularity that is accompanied by a shift from type III
collagen to type I collagen, and morphological reorganization and
realignment of the collagen to respond to mechanical loads.
Molecular mediators .  A myriad of cytokines, including IGFs, VEGF,
PDGF and members of the TGFβ family, regulate the production of col-
lagen and extracellular matrix during the reparative and remodelling
phases 129 , 130 . These cytokines induce paracrine and autocrine responses
that affect fibroblast migration, angiogenesis, collagen production
and matrix metalloproteinase activity 131 . Variations in both the quan-
tity and temporal expression of these cytokines markedly affect the
healing response at the enthesis, including the potential to shift from
fibrosis and scar formation to tissue regeneration. Accordingly, there
is a tremendous focus on augmenting tendon–bone healing through
modulation of these growth factors or through a paracrine effect of
secretomes associated with local stem cell recruitment or delivery 131 – 133 .
Diagnosis, screening and prevention
Clinical presentation
Patients with a symptomatic rotator cuff tear typically present at
~50–70 years of age, with pain at night that keeps them from sleeping,
pain with overhead activity such has reaching a high shelf, or a subjec-
tive feeling of shoulder girdle weakness. Despite a clear pathophysi-
ological progression from tendinopathy to partial-thickness tears to
full-thickness tears, symptom severity does not necessarily correlate
with histopathological severity (Fig.  4 ).
Diagnosis
The diagnosis of a rotator cuff tear is based on the location of pain
(typically along the lateral shoulder), timing of pain (nocturnal
pain and pain with overhead activities) and key physical examination
findings. However, the location and type of pain described by patients
are often non-specific and only beneficial to a certain extent. Under-
standing the symptoms of rotator cuff pathology in comparison with
other common shoulder ailments, especially frozen shoulder and gle-
nohumeral arthritis, is important. The differential diagnosis of rotator
cuff tears is broad and should include degenerative conditions of the
shoulder girdle and the cervical spine (Table  1 ). Physical examination
of the patients suspected to have rotator cuff pathology should focus
on the integrity and functioning of the rotator cuff and periscapular
muscles. Despite advances in imaging techniques, history and physical
examination remain the primary diagnostic tools and determinants
of management.
Physical examination .  Physical examination must begin by expos-
ing both shoulder girdles to assess their symmetry. Muscle atrophy,
in the infraspinatus or supraspinatus fossae, is common in patients
with chronic, larger rotator cuff tears 134 . The acromioclavicular joint,
anterior capsule, coracoid process, posterior capsule, bicipital groove
and acromion are palpated for tenderness 135 . Most patients with rotator
cuff tears will not report tenderness in these areas, but they should be
assessed for concomitant pathology.
A comprehensive assessment of shoulder range of motion is per-
formed. The cervical spine should be examined to rule out pain coming
primarily from a spine condition. Cervical spine pathology typically
presents with a history of radiating pain down past the shoulder and
into the hand, or associated numbness and tingling in a neurological
distribution. During examination of the shoulder, forward flexion,
abduction, internal rotation and external rotation are evaluated from
the adducted position (that is, with the patient’s arm at the side). Inter-
nal and external rotation should also be performed with the shoulder
abducted to 90° with the elbow flexed to 90° to check for early arthritis
and frozen shoulder — both conditions cause loss of active and passive
motion 136 .
The strength of the rotator cuff muscle is also assessed at this step.
Abduction strength is tested with the patient’s arms abducted at 90°

and forward-flexed at 30° to be in the plane of the supraspinatus 137 .
Weakness in this position (measured manually with applied downward
pressure) can be indicative of a supraspinatus tear. External rotation
strength is tested with the patient’s arm adducted and the elbow flexed
to 90°. Weakness with external rotation can be indicative of a tear of the
infraspinatus and/or teres minor, which is almost always accompanied
by a supraspinatus tear that has extended posteriorly. The patient can
also be assessed for a painful arc — the patient must raise the arms above
the head and bring the arms down slowly to the sides. In a positive
test, the patient reports pain during the 120° movement down to the
40° angle. This test is the most sensitive in patients with a rotator cuff
problem; however, the test cannot differentiate between tendinopathy,
partial-thickness tear and full-thickness tear 138 .
Physical examination tests .  Although several well-described tests
can aid the examiner in diagnosing rotator cuff pathology. These
tests lack high sensitivity and specificity and, therefore, must be used
in conjunction with history and imaging to establish an accurate diag-
nosis. The Neer test is performed with the examiner standing behind
the patient with the affected arm passively elevated with the examiner’s
hand stabilizing the scapula. Pain at maximal forward flexion is often
indicative of rotator cuff pathology 137 , 139 . In the Hawkins–Kennedy test,
the affected arm is passively elevated to 90° of forward flexion and the
elbow is placed at 90° of flexion in the neutral position. The forearm is
then turned into internal rotation, which brings the greater tuberosity
into contact with the undersurface of the acromion. Pain with internal
rotation is suggestive of rotator cuff pathology 137 , 140 . The Jobe test is
performed to assess the strength of the supraspinatus. The patient
places the arm at 90° of abduction and 30° of flexion in the plane of
the scapula. Shoulder elevation is manually resisted by the examiner.
Pain or weakness denotes a positive test 140 , 141 . Furthermore, the drop
arm test can also be used to assess the supraspinatus muscle. The
patient’s arm is passively abducted to 90° and the patient then slowly
lowers the straight arm to the side (adducted position). Inability to
control the descent of the arm or pain reported during this manoeuvre
constitutes a positive test 138 – 140 .
Several tests are also available to assess the subscapularis muscle
and tendon. The shoulder must be placed in an internally rotated
position prior to testing to eliminate the strength of the pectoralis.
The lift-off test (Gerber test) is performed by placing the arm in an
internal rotated position behind the patient’s back 138 . The dorsum of the
hand is placed at the base of the lumbar spine and the patient is asked
to lift the hand off the back and hold the position. The strength of the
manoeuvre is assessed by the examiner by applying counter-pressure
to the hand. An inability to lift the hand off the back or inability to
resist the examiner’s counter-pressure represents a positive test 140 , 141 .
The belly press test evaluates subscapularis function: in a standing or
seated position, the patient’s arm is abducted ~20–30° with the arm
internally rotated and elbow flexed to 90°. The patient is instructed
to press the palm of the hand into the abdomen while keeping the ipsi-
lateral elbow in the abducted starting position. The patient not being
able to perform the manoeuvre or the elbow falling to the patient’s side
during the attempted press indicates a positive test 140 , 142 , 143 .
No single test is diagnostically accurate, but the combination of
manoeuvres, and correlation with symptoms, facilitates an accurate
diagnosis of rotator cuff pathology. However, distinguishing the spec-
trum of impingement to partial-thickness to small full-thickness tears
remains challenging. Nevertheless, large tears and those with loss of
the force couple from tears that extend through the rotator cuff cable 55
are readily diagnosed owing to profound weakness of the shoulder
(pseudoparesis) and by a positive external rotation lag sign 138 , 144 .
Radiography .  Imaging is an important diagnostic tool for evaluating
the severity of rotator cuff pathology. Multiple imaging modalities
allow non-invasive diagnosis of rotator cuff tears and can provide key
information to determine the most appropriate and effective treatment
options. Imaging evaluation should begin with plain radiographs of
the shoulder, as they are inexpensive and accessible, and can provide
information regarding acute bony injury or confirm the presence
of concomitant degenerative joint disease, which influences initial
management. Key findings from imaging include the degree of rotator
cuff tear arthropathy 145 ; degenerative or proliferative changes at the

MRI .  MRI offers a comprehensive three-dimensional evaluation of
the rotator cuff and has high diagnostic performance in detecting
partial-thickness and full-thickness rotator cuff tears, with sensitivity
and specificity values ranging from 0.8 to 0.97 (refs.  154 , 155 ). Images
from MRI can provide an accurate assessment of clinically relevant
measurements that help predict the likely success of surgical repair,
including tear size 156  and tendon retraction 157 , and depict the pres-
ence of associated injuries of the acromioclavicular joint, labrum and
glenohumeral cartilage 158 . A fat-sensitive sagittal–oblique sequence
is essential for evaluating the rotator cuff muscle quality, which is
most commonly performed clinically with the Fuchs modification of
the Goutallier classification 159 . The Goutallier classification grades
muscle quality from 0 to 4: 0 indicates normal muscle with no fat,
1 indicates streaks of fat in the muscle, 2 indicates more muscle than
fat, 3 indicates equal proportions of muscle and fat, and 4 indicates
more fat than muscle in the expected muscle location 159 . Muscle quality,
especially of the infraspinatus, is predictive of surgical outcomes after
rotator cuff repair 160 – 162 . The Goutallier classification impacts clinical
decisions, although the reproducibility of this system is moderate at
best 163 – 165 . Advanced MRI techniques, such as IDEAL MRI and Dixon fat–
water separation sequences, can allow a more precise measurement of
intramuscular fat that allows improved treatment recommendations
compared with findings from conventional MRI 89 , 166 – 169 . Information
obtained from preoperative MRI can be utilized in scoring systems,
such as the rotator cuff healing index, which offer insight into the
likelihood of success after surgical treatment 170 . Magnetic resonance
arthrography has excellent diagnostic performance and is an additional
option for imaging, although the addition of intra-articular contrast
material is often not necessary for the routine evaluation of rotator
cuff tears 155 , 171 . In areas with limited MRI availability, availability of
arthroscopic surgical resources might also be limited, making overall
diagnosis and treatment of rotator cuff pathology very challenging.
CT .  CT is less frequently utilized than other imaging modalities for
the diagnosis of rotator cuff tears. The Goutallier classification was
originally developed based on CT imaging. Although CT can provide an
evaluation of muscle quality, a detailed analysis of soft tissue employing
a CT scan is limited 161 . For patients who do not have access to MRI, a CT
arthrogram can be an option to evaluate the rotator cuff; extension of
contrast material into the subacromial space indicates a full-thickness
rotator cuff tear 172 .
Ultrasonography .  Ultrasonography can offer excellent information
regarding all clinically relevant aspects of rotator cuff injuries, while
also being low-cost, portable and more comfortable for patients
than MRI 173 – 175 . Ultrasonography can detect partial-thickness and
full-thickness rotator cuff tears, with specificity ranging from 0.77 to
0.98 (refs.  155 , 176 , 177 ). Ultrasonography can be utilized to determine
rotator cuff tear size, retraction and muscle quality 16 , 178 . Although
ultrasonographic evaluation can be operator-dependent, this modal-
ity can be an excellent, low-cost first-line option for evaluating the
rotator cuff 173 .
Prevention strategies
When developing prevention strategies for the development of rota-
tor cuff tears, it is important to understand that not all risk factors
are modifiable. However, strategies should minimize not only the
development of rotator cuff pathology but also symptoms. Postural
training, flexibility programmes and simple strengthening exercises
for the shoulder and scapular muscles can be beneficial in improving
shoulder health 179 . These strategies might help to minimize the risk of
developing poor posture or form, which can lead to compensation and
undue pressure on an ageing rotator cuff, leading to injury. However,
studies have not shown a clear link between improving posture and
minimizing the incidence of cuff tear and, therefore, the benefits of
better posture remain theoretical.
Although flexibility and rotator cuff exercises can be beneficial in
the prevention of symptoms, studies have shown that excessive repeti-
tive use of the rotator cuff can be detrimental 180 . The repetitive nature
of overhead sports is a well-known factor that contributes to rota-
tor cuff symptoms, although how progression to full-thickness tears
occurs is unclear. Limiting the time spent engaged in repetitive exer-
cises and activities, careful biomechanical evaluation of performance
and a continual emphasis on form are the most effective ­preventative
measures to avoid symptoms arising from a rotator cuff tear.
Smoking cessation programmes are imperative before any surgi-
cal intervention to circumvent its negative effect on healing. Chronic

and obesity can contribute to tendon injury 181 . Although no clear dietary
change can prevent rotator cuff tears, following an anti-inflammatory

Management
The goals of management are to reduce pain and enable return to
function, including improved sleep. In asymptomatic patients or in
those with minimal shoulder symptoms, observation alone might be
sufficient. These patients should be made well aware of the risks of
progression, with pain serving as a key marker of likely tear ­progression
over time and an indicator warranting evaluation and treatment.
Physical therapy
The predominant treatment modality in patients with symptomatic rota-
tor cuff tendinopathy, or partial-thickness or full-thickness tears is phys-
ical therapy. Physical therapy is effective and when successful, results
in quicker return to activities and function than surgical treatment 185 .
The MOON Shoulder Group followed 381 patients (31–90 years
of age; mean age 62 years) with atraumatic full-thickness rotator cuff
tears over 2 years, and demonstrated substantial improvements in
patient-reported outcomes (PROs) including American Shoulder
and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) scores and a reduced rate of surgery after
physical therapy 186 . Patients undertook 6–12 weeks of a standardized
supervised physical therapy programme focusing on basic rotator cuff
strengthening and shoulder mobility, and >70% of the patients demon-
strated improvements in PROs and were satisfied with non-operative
management. Importantly, belief that physical therapy would improve
their symptoms was the most important factor in predicting the suc-
cess of therapy 187 . Several other randomized studies have shown similar
outcomes comparing physical therapy and surgical intervention with
short-term and mid-term follow-up 188 , 189 , although long-term PROs may
favour surgery in some patients at 10 years after presentation 190 . Even
in the setting of massive tears, a deltoid muscle rehabilitation regimen
is effective in improving function and pain 191 .
The goal of physical therapy is restoration of full, pain-free
range of motion, flexibility, muscle balance and scapulothoracic and
glenohumeral muscular control and stability. Improving scapular
stability, proper neuromuscular control of the shoulder girdle and
thoracic posture are essential in a well-designed rotator cuff exercise
programme 192 , 193 . Potential contributing mechanisms to abnormal
scapular kinematics include pain, soft-tissue tightness, altered muscle
activation or strength imbalances, muscle fatigue and poor thoracic
posture 194 , 195 . Studies have shown tightness of the pectoralis minor
and posterior glenohumeral capsular stiffness in relation to abnormal
scapular position. Rehabilitation exercises to stretch the posterior cuff
and pectoralis minor and reduce upper trapezius activation are recom-
mended to improve soft-tissue tightness and allow optimal scapular
motion. For larger tears, physiotherapy should focus on active anterior
deltoid strengthening, teres minor strengthening for active external
rotation, scapular stability and control exercises, patient education,
adaptation, proprioception and a home exercise programme 191 .
Medical management
Pharmacotherapy, including medications such as acetaminophen,
NSAIDs and corticosteroids, can be used for short-term pain relief in
patients with symptomatic rotator cuff tears. If no contraindications
exist, acetaminophen is commonly used as the first-line medication and
NSAIDs are suggested for intermittent use as a second-line treatment.
Oral and topical NSAIDs can effectively relieve shoulder pain secondary
to tear if used for a short time period (4–8 weeks) 196 , 197  and have been
shown to be safe preoperatively and postoperatively 198 . The adverse
effects of these medications should be taken into consideration when
used as long-term treatment.
Subacromial corticosteroid injections (CSIs) are recommended
in the inflammatory stage or early stages of rotator cuff tears to
reduce pain and improve function in patients with moderate-to-
severe pain and in those who failed initial physical therapy and/or
oral anti-inflammatory drugs. CSIs can be effective in the short term,
especially for small tears, and CSIs in combination with physical therapy
can successfully reduce pain and improve function within 6 weeks.
This combination can allow some patients to avoid surgery altogether.
If not successful after 6 weeks of treatment, the long-term efficacy of
CSIs is limited. Importantly, preoperative CSIs may be associated with
increased rates of postoperative infections and revision surgery if
administered within 1 month of surgery 199 – 201 . Furthermore, the admin-
istration of two or more CSIs the year before surgery substantially
increases the risk of subsequent revision rotator cuff surgery 199 .
Platelet-rich plasma
Many studies have investigated platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as a potential
non-operative treatment for rotator cuff tears and have shown mixed
results regarding the effectiveness of these injections. The studies
showed reduced pain and improved function in patients with tendi-
nopathy at 6 months with deterioration to baseline levels by 1 year 202 .
A meta-analysis in 2023 demonstrated that PRP was equivalent to CSIs
in terms of pain relief and function at any given time point 203 . In addi-
tion, these studies showed substantial variability in the number of PRP
injections, injection volume and platelet concentration. According to
the 2019 clinical practice guidelines from the American Academy of
Orthopaedic Surgeons, evidence is limited to support the routine use
of PRP for treating cuff tendinopathy or partial-thickness tears and,
currently, PRP is not indicated for the treatment of full-thickness tears.
Mesenchymal stem cells
The advent of MSCs has opened new potential avenues for therapeutic
interventions for rotator cuff tears. This technique involves the injec-
tion of MSCs into the rotator cuff tendon or into the subacromial bursa.
Studies have shown significant improvements in pain and functional
outcomes in patients treated with MSCs compared with placebo, but
further research assessing mid-term and long-term outcomes is needed
to standardize their use 204 . Moreover, the use of culture-expanded
MSCs is not approved by the FDA and, therefore, these cellular treat-
ments are only available in the USA within an Investigational New Drug
application or FDA-regulated randomized control trial.
Proper patient counselling and careful patient selection are crucial
when considering non-operative management of rotator cuff tears.
Educating the patient to understand that non-operative treatment

typically does not result in a complete ‘cure’ or healing of the tear
despite symptom improvement is important. Although conservative
management can successfully treat most patients with rotator cuff
tears, ~25% of patients progress to need surgical intervention.
Surgical intervention
Subacromial decompression including acromioplasty is a widely per-
formed procedure to treat subacromial impingement syndrome 205 , 206 .
Despite the theoretical advantages of acromioplasty, its role in pain
management and/or prevention of progression to rotator cuff tear is still
a matter of debate. Several randomized trials found superiority of acro -
mioplasty to exercise therapy alone 207 , 208 ; however, long-term follow-up
studies found no significant differences in ­clinical outcomes 209 – 213 .
All-arthroscopic rotator cuff repair is considered the treatment of
choice for rotator cuff tears that fail non-operative management or for
acute traumatic rotator cuff tears. However, retear remains an unsolved
problem, reported for 10–40% of procedures 214 . Short-term outcomes
are favourable regardless of retear; however, in many patients with
retear, long-term outcomes are worse than in patients without retear
and deteriorate over time 215 – 217 .
To prevent retear, accurate prediction of reparability and healing
after repair is important. Predictors of retear can be classified as tear
characteristic factors and patient demographic factors. Tear charac-
teristic factors include tear size 170 , 218 – 220 , fatty infiltration and muscle
atrophy 221 . These factors could decrease the elasticity and vitality of
the rotator cuff tendon tissues, inhibiting healing after rotator cuff
repair 221 , 222 . Old age, diabetes, osteoporosis, dyslipidaemia, obesity
and smoking are demographic risk factor for retear 170 , 218 , 222 – 225 . Using a
15-point scoring system to predict healing after cuff repair 170  (Table  2 ),
retear rate in patients with ≤4 points was 6.0%; however, rates in those
with ≥5 and ≥10 points were 55.2% and 86.2%, respectively.
Given the high retear rates, there has been considerable focus on
improving the biomechanical and biological construct of the repair. The
ideal biomechanical suture configuration for rotator cuff repair
requires mechanical stability until tendon-to-bone healing occurs,
without excessive tension, which can damage the repaired tendon 226 , 227 .
To achieve this goal, several repair techniques are employed but there
is no consensus on the optimal technique, which is likely to depend on
tear configuration and so-far-undefined patient-specific factors 228 .
Although the biomechanical properties and retear rates of double-row
suture bridge repair are better than simple single-row repair 229 – 231 ,
clinical outcomes according to repair technique are not significantly
different 232 .
The use of orthobiologics, such as MSCs intraoperatively has
shown promising results to decrease retear rates 233 . In a prospective
study 234 , bone marrow aspirate concentrate augmentation resulted in
a 39% decrease in retear rate at 1 year compared with a control group
who underwent rotator cuff repair only. For PRP, several studies have
shown improved healing rates, particularly in larger tears, but clinical
outcomes are not improved by PRP augmentation 235 – 238 . Although PRP
may enable some early mobilization and pain relief, the long-term
outcomes are comparable to those following well-performed rota-
tor cuff repair 239 , 240 . The clinical evidence for the use of PRP in the
non-operative management of rotator cuff disorders is inconsistent,
making inferences difficult. Furthermore, in one study in patients with
rotator cuff tears managed with arthroscopic surgical repair, there was
no difference in clinical and imaging outcomes at 10 years follow-up
between those receiving and those not receiving PRP injections 241 . The
wide range of effectiveness can be related to the types of PRP used and
the variability in preparation techniques, cell counts and growth factors
available 242 . These differences make it necessary to plan adequately
powered appropriately standardized studies with clinically relevant
outcome measures.
In patients with an irreparable tear, when retracted tendons can-
not be re-attached to the greater or lesser tuberosity without arthritic
change of the glenohumeral joint, other treatment options are con-
sidered, especially in young, active patients. These include partial
repair, superior capsular reconstruction (reconstructing the superior
capsule using allograft or autogenic tensor fascia lata), an allogenic or
xenogenic patch bridging graft, muscle advancement (releasing the
proximal rotator cuff muscle to re-attach to the footprint), tendon
transfer, subacromial balloon spacer, and other procedures 243 – 246 .
Results with these diverse treatment strategies are mixed, as expected
in small case series with complex conditions.
If the rotator cuff tear is assumed to be irreparable in older
patients and/or if accompanied by arthritic change of the gleno-
humeral joint, reverse shoulder replacement is the primary treatment
option 247 . Reverse shoulder arthroplasty reverses normal anatomy, so
that the glenoid has the ‘ball’ side (glenosphere on the glenoid side)
articulating with the humeral cup (metal tray with polyethylene insert).
This technique has excellent long-term outcomes for irreparable cuff
tears and cuff tear arthropathy 247 , 248 . The 10-year success rate of reverse
shoulder arthroplasty is up to 93%, but functional outcomes deterio-
rate over time, particularly in patients >80 years of age 249 – 251 . Various
complications related to reverse shoulder including scapular acro-
mial notching, instability, infection and glenoid loosening can occur
An overall algorithm for treatment is presented in Fig.  5 . Rotator
cuff repairs with concomitant moderate-to-severe glenohumeral osteo-
arthritis had worse outcomes than rotator cuff tears with mild gleno-
humeral osteoarthritis 258 . Thus, reverse shoulder arthroplasty in patients
with rotator cuff tear with concomitant moderate-to-severe osteoar-
thritis classified as grade ≥2 using the Samilson–Prieto ­classification
is the treatment of choice, especially in those >65 years old 148 , 258 .

sification grade ≤1) 148 , the healing potential of the rotator cuff repair is

Quality of life
Rotator cuff tears of all sizes and cuff tear arthropathy can be painful
with the potential to compromise motion, strength and function 261 , 262 .
By contrast, structural change to the rotator cuff can be identified in a
large proportion of individuals (~60 years of age) who never complain
of shoulder pain or loss of function 263 . Hence, the effect of rotator cuff
disease on well-being remains difficult to analyse.
Psychosocial and mental health problems are associated with
greater symptoms and worse function in patients with symptomatic
rotator cuff disease than in patients without mental health issues 33 , 264 , 265 .
Whether the rotator cuff disease is further disabling because of worse
mental coping mechanisms or if the chronicity of rotator cuff disease
leads to psychological and mental dysfunction in these individuals
remains unclear. Regardless of causation, anxiety, depression and
similar disorders seem to negatively impact non-operative and surgi-
cal outcomes. In addition, rotator cuff disease is associated with sleep
disturbance 266 – 268 , which is common even more than a year after rotator
cuff repair 269 .
Long-term outcomes
Non-surgical management .  Relatively few studies have investigated
the long-term quality of life and overall outcomes in patients treated
non-operatively for full-thickness rotator cuff tear. For example, in
one study that followed 93 patients for five or more years, 25 patients
(27%) had undergone surgery at the latest follow-up. Of 63 patients
who completed the rotator cuff quality of life (RC-QOL) questionnaire,
those treated non-operatively scored 83 out of 100 points and those
treated surgically scored 89 out of 100 (ref.  270 ). Isolated full-thickness
supraspinatus tears seem to respond well to non-operative manage-
ment in the long term, with reasonable overall outcomes and lim-
ited progression of fatty infltration 271 . Tear progression seems to be
associated with recurrent or deteriorating symptoms and, therefore,
some patients who initially respond well to non-surgical treatment
may undergo surgery in the future. In a long-term randomized con-
trolled trial, the non-operative group did not do as well as the surgically
repaired group at 10 years, suggesting that, in younger or more active
patients, repair may be preferred 190 , a concept that was supported in
a propensity-matched study 272 .
Surgical rotator cuff repair .  Several studies have shown substantial
improvements in pain, function and quality of life following rotator cuff
repair 261 , 273 , 274 . Improvements in quality of life after surgery seem to be
maintained in the long term 261 , 275 . Interestingly, and rather perplexing,
a large number of patients who became and remained asymptomatic
after rotator cuff repair showed evidence of persistent thinning of
the repaired site or even structural failure of the rotator cuff repair on

Fig. 5 | Algorithm for rotator cuff tear treatment.
Patients with a known rotator cuff tear are evaluated
with radiographs. Patients with advanced arthritis
(Samilson–Prieto (S-P) grade 2–4) are treated
with either conservative management (physical
therapy or steroid injections) or reverse shoulder
arthroplasty (RSA). In patients with minimal arthritis
(S-P grade 1), treatment is determined by the rotator
cuff healing index (RoHI) 170 . A score of >5/15 suggests
a lower likelihood of healing, and the acromial
humeral distance (AHD) is measured as a sign of
chronic superior humeral head migration. If the AHD
is <3.2 mm, then surgical treatment should be an
RSA. In patients with a RoHI score of <5/15, or an AHD
of >3.2 mm, a rotator cuff repair is recommended.
Small tears can be treated with several options
including simple sutures or a modified Mason–
Allen repair. For larger tears and massive tears,
greater tuberosity (GT) coverage dictates either
a double row suture bridge or single row repair to
reduce tension on the repair. Irreparable tears can
be treated with RSA, tendon transfers or another
procedure such as a biceps transfer or balloon
arthroplasty.

Several long-term studies suggest that healing is important and, if
repair integrity is intact, the outcomes at 10–15 years after surgery
are excellent 276 , 277 .
Reverse shoulder arthroplasty .  Reverse shoulder arthroplasty pro-
vides substantial improvements in pain, function and quality of life in
many individuals with an irreparable cuff tear, particularly cuff tear
arthropathy 278 , 279 . Reverse shoulder arthroplasty is particularly useful
when the subscapularis and the posterosuperior cuff are irreparable,
as well as in the presence of associated arthritis. However, a subset
of patients — individuals with an irreparable cuff tear, no arthritis
and good preoperative active motion — are particularly at risk of
­dissatisfaction after reverse arthroplasty 280 .
Outlook
Several important unmet needs remain in the field of rotator cuff
degeneration and repair (Box  3 ). Much of the focus of ongoing research
revolves around using orthobiologics such as PRP, growth factors,
MSCs and bone marrow aspirate concentrate, to improve tendon–bone
healing and tendinopathy. Preliminary findings from experimental
models and clinical studies are encouraging but are in the early stages
of research 281 , 282 .
A population of MSCs reside in the subacromial bursa, which are
responsive to inflammatory stimuli, indicating an as-yet-unexplored
biological role in the context of rotator cuff disease 283 . Bursa–tendon
crosstalk has an interesting clinical relevance and could be a novel
target in the management of shoulder disorders. Indeed, using a rat
model of rotator cuff injury and repair, proteomic profiling of the
bursa demonstrated increased cytokine expression and MSC pro-
liferation after rotator cuff tears, probably to protect the adjacent
intact tissue and to maintain the morphology of the underlying bone 65 .
The bursa, therefore, may have an initiating and essential role in heal-
ing by acting as a potential cellular source to promote healing after
repair 81 .
Collagen injections have been used to treat rotator cuff tendi-
nopathy and to support arthroscopic repair. Their mechanism of action
is not completely understood, but they have been hypothesized to
induce regenerative pathways, to stimulate tenocyte proliferation and
migration, and to increase endogenous collagen synthesis to restore
collagen fibres in damaged tendons 284 – 287 . Integrin receptors in fibro-
blasts are activated and, consequently, the growth factor cascade
initiates the synthesis of endogenous collagen, healing the damaged
collagen fibres and leading to proper alignment 288 – 292 . Despite the use
of different types of collagens and injection protocols, or applying
different PROs, studies have shown clinically and functionally relevant
improvements.
Novel surgical procedures to improve outcomes of rotator cuff
repair are consistently being attempted, but management of large
and massive tears remains a considerable challenge. In patients with
massive rotator cuff tears (>5 cm), repair of the superior capsule as well
as the rotator cuff has been reported to improve retear rates at early
follow up 293 , 294 . The superior capsule defect has been defined as the
essential lesion of rotator cuff tears 295 , and several researchers recom-
mend its repair to restore shoulder biomechanics, preventing the rise
of the humeral head in the postoperative period 296 , 297 . It is also thought
to provide an additional source of autologous tenocytes, which may
improve the biology of the repair.
The long head of the biceps tendon, that is rich in tenocytes and
fibroblasts, is a major stabilizer of the humeral head preventing supe-
rior migration and reinforcing repair 298 – 300 . Its use for cuff repair may
provide a local autologous source of collagen, and can be incorporated
in the repair arthroscopically with no further surgical exposure or
donor site morbidity 298 .
Optimal management of shoulder disability may be influenced
by the more detailed understanding of the natural history of disease
and the anticipated operative outcomes. Machine learning employs
computer algorithms and statistical analysis to determine complex
trends and patterns, which can be difficult to perform in a clinical set-
ting. Studies have shown promising results in accurately predicting
postoperative scores. This methodology may be improved to support
preoperative counselling, planning and resource allocation 301 .
Overall, rotator cuff pathology is a substantial clinical problem
with room for improvements in our understanding of pathophysiology,
and future research should focus on methods to optimize outcomes
of treatment.
